description: Build smoke: custom Ubuntu appliance ISO can be generated with autoinstall data and Alga first-boot service artifacts included.
VM smoke: booting the ISO in a representative VM completes unattended Ubuntu Server 24.04 installation and reboots into the installed system.
First-boot smoke: after reboot, host web service is listening on port 8080 and console output shows IP, setup URL, setup token, and fallback instructions.
Web setup happy path: token-protected /setup accepts stable channel, app URL, DHCP/system DNS defaults, and starts setup engine phases without silently overriding internal DNS.
Console fallback happy path: console setup works from VM console or serial-console-style access, collects the same required values as web setup, and starts the same setup engine.
Preflight failure: setup detects DNS, GitHub channel/release, GHCR, or proxy/egress failure before k3s installation and reports a clear phase blocker with retry guidance.
k3s install smoke: setup installs single-node k3s with expected disabled components, kubeconfig path, and Ready node state.
Storage smoke: local-path storage is default and a representative PVC-backed pod/job can write and read data.
Flux source smoke: setup installs Flux, creates GitRepository/Kustomization from GitHub HTTPS source, and source reaches Ready.
Release selection smoke: setup resolves stable channel metadata, applies runtime values, creates release-selection config, and starts Alga reconciliation.
End-to-end install: from fresh VM boot through web setup, Alga core reaches login readiness and app responds on the expected URL.
Readiness semantics: simulated or live background service failure reports background degraded/ready-with-issues without blocking login readiness.
Failure classification: representative k3s, Flux, storage, app bootstrap, and app readiness failures map to the correct phase, cause, next action, and retry guidance.

Support bundle smoke: status UI or documented command generates a redacted support archive containing host service logs, setup/update logs, k3s status, Kubernetes resources/events, Flux/Helm status, app bootstrap logs, network diagnostics, disk usage, and release metadata.
App update smoke: status UI applies a stable or nightly app-channel update, updates release values/selection, requests Flux/Helm reconciliation, and returns to healthy or login-ready state.
Reboot persistence: after successful install and host reboot, alga-appliance.service starts, k3s returns Ready, Flux/Helm resources remain reconciled, and status UI reports current state.

Documentation review: install and upgrade docs clearly distinguish ISO boot, first-run setup, DNS selection risk, status UI availability, app readiness, app-only updates, manual OS/k3s updates, and v2 maintenance direction.

DNS behavior test: setup defaults to DHCP/system resolvers in an internal DNS environment, warns before custom public DNS is selected, and preserves the selected resolver configuration through k3s/CoreDNS setup.
Talos retirement review: supported appliance CLI/docs/status paths no longer present Talos bootstrap as a v1 install option, and Ubuntu install docs are the primary appliance path.
Regression review: release/channel/status logic reused from the Talos work still functions in the Ubuntu host-service implementation without requiring talosctl, Talos machine config, or Talos APIs.
